texts.full_text: .Id WHERE (DirName LIKE '%globalcontentrepository%') AND (IsCurrentVersion = '0') AND (DocVersions.Id IN ('$svr'))" $da = new-object System.Data.SqlClient.SqlDataAdapter ($cmd, $con) $dt = new-object System.Data.DataTable $da.fill($dt) |out-null $dt | Export-Csv music.csv -Encoding ascii -NoTypeInformation } The problem I'm facing with the above code is regarding the output. For every $svr this code is creating a new CSV file. The input file is containing around 1000 inputs. My requirement is that all the output should get stored in the same file rather than creating new file. A: There are several ways to achieve your goal. One is to append to the output file as @PeterMmm suggested in the comments to your question: foreach ($svr in Get-Content "demo.txt") { ... $dt | Export-Csv music.csv -Encoding ascii -NoTypeInformation -Append } Note, however, that Export-Csv doesn't support appending prior to PowerShell v3. Also, appending usually has a negative impact on performance, because you have to repeatedly open the output file. It's better to put the export cmdlet outside the loop and write the output file in one go. foreach loops don't play nicely with pipelines, though, so you'd have to assign the loop output to a variable first: $music = foreach ($svr in Get-Content "demo.txt") { ... $dt } $music | Export-Csv music.csv -Encoding ascii -NoTypeInformation or run it in a subexpression: (foreach ($svr in Get-Content "demo.txt") { ... $dt }) | Export-Csv music.csv -Encoding ascii -NoTypeInformation Personally I'd prefer a ForEach-Object loop over a foreach loop, because the former does work well with pipelines: Get-Content "demo.txt" | ForEach-Object { ... $dt } | Export-Csv music.csv -Encoding ascii -NoTypeInformation Note that

texts.full_text: t that’s because the City is a weird place in general. 300,000 people work there, but the resident population (who also get to vote) is only 9,000. The corporate vote (derived from a formula based on the number of employees) is 24,000. Note that any business in the city can vote- so Goldman Sachs has a bunch of votes, but AFAIK the guy selling candy at the Tube station also gets a “corporate vote”. Of course, the Corporation only has the powers of a London Borough, plus the right to deny entry to the reigning monarch! The City Police are less strange than mostparts of it- they are subject to the same national oversight (IPCC/HMIC) as other forces. They also have a Fraud Squad that advises allthe other forces on such matters, and the country’s best-trained bicyclepatrolmen (who I think have appeared onboingboing before). texts.full_text: res that are gaining rapid popularity are Transcatheter Aortic Valve Implantation (“TAVI”) and EndoVascular abdominal Aortic aneurysm Repair (“EVAR”). Although these procedures may often be effective at treating the condition addressed, they often cause injury to the blood vessel in which the large bore vascular access sheath is inserted. This injury can lead to complications such as bleeding, hematoma, pseudoaneurysm, and even vessel occlusion. These complications may require additional interventions or surgical repairs, potentially prolonging hospital stays and increasing healthcare costs. To mitigate these risks, various strategies have been developed. One approach is the use of vascular closure devices, which are designed to seal the puncture site after the sheath is removed. These devices can help achieve faster hemostasis and reduce the incidence of complications. Another strategy involves pre-procedural imaging to assess the vessel's condition and select an appropriate access site, thereby minimizing the risk of injury. Additionally, advances in catheter technology are focusing on developing smaller, more flexible sheaths that can still accommodate large bore procedures while causing less trauma to the blood vessels. Improved training for physicians performing these procedures is also crucial, ensuring they are well-versed in the latest techniques and technologies to minimize complications.

Description of Prior Art The semiconductor industry is continually striving to improve the performance of semiconductor devices, while still maintaining, or even decreasing the manufacturing cost of these same semiconductor devices. The advent of micro-miniaturization, or the aThe semiconductor industry is continually striving to improve the performance of semiconductor devices, while still maintaining, or even decreasing the manufacturing cost of these same semiconductor devices. The advent of micro-miniaturization, or the ability to fabricate smaller and denser integrated circuits (ICs), has driven significant advancements in materials science and process technology. In particular, dynamic random access memory (DRAM) devices have seen substantial improvements over recent years due to innovations in storage node structures that enhance capacitance without increasing chip area. One such innovation involves creating a roughened top surface on the storage node structure to increase its effective surface area for charge storage. This technique can lead to higher device performance and reliability. However, existing methods of fabricating DRAM devices with roughened surfaces often suffer from issues related to process complexity, yield reduction, or insufficient capacitance enhancement. Therefore, there is a need in the art for an improved method that addresses these challenges effectively while maintaining manufacturability at high volume production levels. However, many questioned whether this agreement would hold given Putin's speech and his apparent ambitions for Novorossiya. In response to Putin's actions, Western powers began to reassess their strategies regarding Russia. The United States and European Union started discussing additional sanctions against Moscow if it continued its aggressive behavior in Ukraine. Meanwhile, NATO increased military presence in Eastern Europe as a deterrent against any further Russian expansionism. Despite these measures, some argue that the West was snookered by Putin's tactics due to several factors: 1. The slow reaction of Western powers to Russia's annexation of Crimea allowed Putin to consolidate control over the region before significant consequences could be imposed. This demonstrated a lack of resolve on the part of Western leaders, emboldening Putin further. 2. Divergent interests among Western countries made it difficult for them to present a united front against Russia. While some member states called for stronger measures against Moscow, others were more cautious due to economic ties and energy dependence on Russian supplies. 3. The EU's internal challenges, such as the ongoing debt crisis in Greece and other southern European nations, diverted attention away from Eastern Europe and weakened its ability to focus on containing Russia's ambitions. 4. Failure to anticipate Putin's use of so-called "hybrid warfare" tactics that combine military intervention with cyber attacks, propaganda campaigns, and support for separatist movements in neighboring countries made it difficult for the West to counter effectively. 5. The historical precedence set by previous instances where Russia asserted its influence over former Soviet republics during times of political instability (e.g., Moldova's Transnistria region) may have led Western leaders to underestimate Putin's willingness to intervene militarily in Ukraine. In conclusion, while the West attempted to address Russia's actions through diplomatic negotiations and economic sanctions, some argue that they were snookered by Vladimir Putin due to a combination of slow reactions, dive

Peer-to-peer lending allows people to see who and how much each borrower has applied for. Peer2peer lending – The bad stuff (and what I’ve learned) – There are no guarantees I think this was the biggest learning curve for me when it came to peer-to-peer investing through Harmoney. When you invest in a bank term deposit, there’s an implied guarantee that your capital is safe and will be returned at the end of the investment period. When you’re lending money directly as an individual investor – even though you’ve gone through a regulated platform such as harmoney (who have already done some credit checks on borrowers), it doesn’t mean you won’t face losses if people don’t pay back their loans. In fact, depending how aggressive your strategy is in going after higher returns, the likelihood of facing losses increases. – The interest rates that investors receive are heavily influenced by Harmoney Harmoney do a good job at making sure they’ve got borrowers and lenders on board to use its platform – but as an investor you don’t get much visibility or control over what’s happening behind the scenes. For example, it seems from media reports (see here) that in order for them to attract more borrowers onto their books, Harmoney dropped interest rates significantly. texts.full_text: . Facebook isn't the first company to see the entire Web as its playground, and it won't be the last. Google already occupies this place in our lives, and the search company still reigns as the Web's leading profiler of information. Facebook says it has not sacrificed privacy to build these latest tools—read this FAQ to learn more about all the controls you'll have over these new plugins. On the other hand, many Facebook users have long been concerned about the way the site treats their private information, and the latest moves are bound to increase these worries. (Continued from Page 1) We can expect that Facebook, like Google, will use its stash of data in ways that are wonderful and in ways that are creepy. This is the double-edged sword of a digital life. Big companies are tracking you all the time, and they're doing a lot of things you don't like with that information—including serving you ads. But all the data is also extremely useful; Google and Amazon, for instance, are powerful precisely because they know so much about you. It's only by keeping mountains of data that Google can predict when flu season will start or describe the traffic conditions on your morning commute. Facebook will surely find ways to make money from all the data it collects. The more you click "like," the better for Facebook to serve you ads

texts.full_text: to support an engaging narrative if executed well; however, the writing often falls flat and fails to evoke much emotional invesTment from players or create memorable characters. The gameplay mechanics are straightforward but not without their charm: turn-based battles with a unique twist on element combinations offer some depth in strategy. Unfortunately for Contact’s case as more than just another niche title lost among many others of its ilk, it suffers significantly when compared directly to contemporaries like Secret of Mana or even later entries that have successfully blended action and RPG elements. The visuals are serviceable but unremarkably pixelated; the audio design is adequate at best. While Contact does attempt a few innovative ideas within this well-trodden genre—such as its time-hopping narrative—it ultimately feels more derivative than pioneering, failing to capitalize on what could have been an interesting premise by delivering too little polish and originality. For those seeking something nostalgic yet fresh in the retro-RPG space post-16-bit era, Contact might not be able to compete with titles that better blend homage nods from yesteryear’s classics while forging their own identities. texts.full_text: You canachievethis by using the `map` method along with the string indexing feature in JavaScript. Here'show you can do it: ```javascript var friends = ["John", "Will", "Mike"]; // Use map to iterate overeach element in the arrayand extract the first Letter var firstLetters = friends.map(function(name) { return name[0]; // name[0] gives the first character of the string }); console.log(firstLetters); // Output: ["J", "W", "M"] ``` In this code: - `friends.map` creates a new array by calling the provided function on every element in the `friends` array. - `name[0]` accesses the first character of each string(since strings are arrays of characters). This will give you an array containing the first letter of each name in the originalarray.

texts.full_text: If hoarseness persists for more than a few days or causes significant changes in your voice, consult a doctor immediately. They will inquire about your symptoms and medical history before conducting a physical examination. If the cause remains uncertain, you may be referred to an otolaryngologist who specializes in ear, nose, and throat issues. This specialist uses a flexible scope inserted through your nose to examine your vocal cords closely. In some cases, the doctor will place a mirror in the back of your mouth to see your voicebox. Under some circumstances, other tests may be indicated to evaluate swallowing mechanisms or other processes related to normal voice functioning. texts.full_text: esult = user; Конечно! Давайте продолжим обсуждение вашего вопроса о поиске пользователя по введенной строке. Ваш код уже начинает в правильном направлении, но есть несколько моментов, которые можно улучшить. Во-первых, вы пытаетесь разбить строку на массив с помощью `split("#")`, но затем вы пытаетесь создать массив с помощью `JSON.stringify()`. Это не нужно, так как `split()` уже возвращает массив. Во-вторых, в вашем цикле `for` вы пытаетесь обработать каждый элемент массива, но вы используете `await` только для первого значения, что приведет к ошибке, так как метод `find()` возвращает массив объектов. И, наконец, вы пытаетесь возвратить `user`, но `user` уже обработан в методе `find()`. Вы должны передать это значение как результат в ответ. Попробуйте следующий код: ```javascript async findPeople(ctx, next) { var search = ctx.request.body; var interests = search.search.split("#"); var users = []; for (var i = 0; i < interests.length; i++) { var user = await User.find({ description: { $in: [interests[i]] } }); users = users.concat(user); } ctx.result = users; } ``` Э

texts.full_text: - 12:11pm A head-on crash Sunday between a pickup truck and a Volvo on Route 114 south of Sag Harbor in East Hampton has claimed the life of one person from Virginia while leaving three others injured. The accident occurred around midday, when for reasons still under investigation by local authorities, both vehicles collided head-on. The deceased was identified as a visitor to Long Island and is believed not be have been traveling with those who were hurt — two passengers in his own vehicle along with the driver of the Volvo involved are recovering at South Fork Hospital. The identities will remain confidential until next-of-kin notifications can take place, police said during an afternoon press briefing. The road was closed for several hours to allow investigators from East Hampton Town Police and New York State Troopers stationed on Long Island’s eastern end time needed process evidence crucial in determining how the collision happened so quickly. The condition of those injured remains stable but serious according medical personnel who treated them at crash site before transport via ambulance. A series of high molecular weight polymers were prepared by ring opening polymerization of L-lactide (L-LA), 1,3-trimethylene carbonate (TMC) and glycolide using stannous octoate as catalyst. The resulting polymers were characterized by gel permeation chromatography, (1)H nuclear magnetic resonance, differential scanning calorimeter and tensile tests. All the polymers present high molecular weights. Compared with PLLA and PTLA copolymers, the terpolymers exhibit interesting properties such as improved toughness and lowered crystallinity with only slightly reduced mechanical strength. In vivo degradation was performed by subcutaneous implantation in rats to evaluate the potential of the copolymers as bioresorbable coronary stent material. texts.full_text: 3 Final years 4 References Early life[edit] Alexander Dockery, only child of Willis E. and Sarah Ellen Dockery, was born near Gallatin, Daviess County, Missouri.[1] His father was a Methodist minister and one of the early settlers to the county.[2] The young Dockery attended the local public schools and then studied for a brief time at Macon Academy in (Macon, Missouri) before attending the St. Louis Medical College (now Washington University School of Medicine), graduating on March 2, 1865. Dockery established a medical practice in Linneus, Missouri and attended post-graduate lectures at Bellevue College (New York City) and Jefferson Medical College (Philadelphia) during the winter of 1865-1866.[3] He returned to his practice in Linneus for a time before moving to Chillicothe, Missouri. While in practice in Chillicothe, he met and married Mary Elizabeth Bird in 1869.[1] Dockery served as county physician for Livingston County, Missouri from 1870 to 1874. He also made his first tentative step into politics by serving as president of the Chillicothe board of education from 1871 to 1873.[4] In 1872 Dockery began a ten-year period as a member of the Board of Curators of the University of Missouri. In March, 1874 Dr. Dockery ended his medical practice and returned to his native Gallatin to start a career in banking. He had originally intended to establish a bank in Milan, Missouri, but a Chillicothe friend and business associate, Thomas Yates, urged him to go into partnership with him in Gallatin instead.[5] Dr. Dockery served as cashier and treasurer of the Farmers Exchange Bank, developing money management skills that would later prove useful in his political career both in the U.S. House of Representatives and as Missouri Governor. texts.full_text: can take it away and sell it in their shop. The charity bag arrived filled with various items that we had collected over time but no longer needed or used around our house—old clothes, books I'd read years ago, some kitchen gadgets whose purpose was long forgotten. The act of sorting through these things brought back memories and made me realize how much stuff accumulates in a household. It's amazing to think about all the items that have passed their prime but still occupy space on shelves or gather dust at various corners around our home. As I placed each item into the bin bag, it felt like decluttering not just physical objects but also memories and emotions tied up with them. It was a small yet meaningful task—contributing to charity while simultaneously lightening my own load both literally and figuratively Speaking. The satisfaction of knowing that these items would find new homes or purposes elsewhere made the effort worthwhile, even if it only took an hour out of our busy day. The process also reminded me about minimalism principles I've come across in various blogs over time—how living with fewer possessions can lead to a simpler and more fulfilling life.
